coredata.dc:title: Amide-decorated carbon dots as sensitive and selective probes for fluorescence enhancement detection of cadmium ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Lu
Zhu, Chenxue
Yang, Yingsang
Luo, Jiajun
Song, Jinhui
Chen, Huimin
Liu, Suyao
Liu, Yi
Fang, Yiwen
coredata.dc:description: As highly toxic metal ions, cadmium ions (Cd2+) are prevalent in varying concentrations around the world. The establishment of an accurate and effective method for Cd2+ determination with high sensitivity and selectivity is of particular concern. The present work fabricated a fluorescence chemosensor for the detection of Cd2+ based on functionalized carbon dots (CDs), which were hydrothermally prepared using amidated hyperbranched-polyethyleneimine (HPEI). As investigated by FTIR, NMR, and XPS, the stably grafted amide groups endowed the CDs with thermosensitivity and high cloud point due to the change in hydrophilic-hydrophobic behaviors. The CDs chemosensor with optimal amidation degree exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability in the determination of Cd2+ from various water environments. Notably, the fluorescence intensity enhanced with the increase of Cd2+ concentration, originating from the improved structure rigidity caused by the interactions between grafted amides and Cd2+. These impressive features made the CDs not only sensitive to detecting Cd2+ in low-concentration solutions with a limit of detection of 3.41 nM (the lowest known value for Cd2+ detection) but also accurate for the quantification in high-concentration solutions with a detectable Cd2+ concentration of 6.0 × 10−2 M. Owing to the broad detection range, the CDs developed in present work show great potential applications in various scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amidation
Carbon dots
Fluorescence enhancement
Cd2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cd2+ under different conditions
Determination of Cd2+ in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22072174 ) and the Start-up Funding of Shantou University (No. NTF22013 ). Financial supports from the Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory ( 2111002 ) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2023A1515012115 ) for conducting this research are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR (a) of HPEI, HPEI-IBAm0.65, and CDs-2 samples before and after Cd2+ introduction. 1H NMR spectra (b and c) of HPEI and HPEI-IBAm0.65, as well as the CDs-2 sample. TEM image of the CDs-2 before (d) and after (e) Cd2+ introduction.
Fluorescence intensity (a) of the CDs samples with different amidation degrees. Effect of temperature on the light absorbance (b) of different samples in deionized water.

Fluorescence selectivity (a) of CDs-2 dispersion in detecting various metal ions (6 × 10−2 M). Fluorescence emission spectra (b) of CDs-2 dispersion in deionized water solution of Cd2+ with the increase of Cd2+ concentrations (from bottom to top: 0–6 × 10−2 M). Linear relationship (c) of the ratios of fluorescence intensity and Cd2+ concentrations in the range of 6 × 10−8 to 6 × 10−6 M. Detection fluctuation (d) of the CDs-2 sample in the solution of Cd2+ with a concentration of 6 × 10−3 M. (e) The relationship between the F/F0 values with the detection time from 10 min to 8 h.

Fluorescence emission spectra (a)of CDs-2 dispersion in tap water solution of Cd2+ with increased metal concentrations (from bottom to top: 0–6 × 10−2 M). Linear relationship (b) of the F/F0 ratios and Cd2+ concentrations in the range of 6 × 10−8 to 6 × 10−6 M using tap water as solvents.
Schematic presentation of the synthesis procedure of amide-decorated CDs.
The degrees of amidation, precursors, and Tcp values for various CDs samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Degree of amidation means the mole percentage of amide groups grafted on HPEI or CDs moiety, which is calculated from 1H NMR.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tcp represents the cloud point temperature obtained from the aqueous solution of CDs.

head.ce:title: Amide-decorated carbon dots as sensitive and selective probes for fluorescence enhancement detection of cadmium ion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory, Shantou 515031, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As highly toxic metal ions, cadmium ions (Cd2+) are prevalent in varying concentrations around the world. The establishment of an accurate and effective method for Cd2+ determination with high sensitivity and selectivity is of particular concern. The present work fabricated a fluorescence chemosensor for the detection of Cd2+ based on functionalized carbon dots (CDs), which were hydrothermally prepared using amidated hyperbranched-polyethyleneimine (HPEI). As investigated by FTIR, NMR, and XPS, the stably grafted amide groups endowed the CDs with thermosensitivity and high cloud point due to the change in hydrophilic-hydrophobic behaviors. The CDs chemosensor with optimal amidation degree exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability in the determination of Cd2+ from various water environments. Notably, the fluorescence intensity enhanced with the increase of Cd2+ concentration, originating from the improved structure rigidity caused by the interactions between grafted amides and Cd2+. These impressive features made the CDs not only sensitive to detecting Cd2+ in low-concentration solutions with a limit of detection of 3.41 nM (the lowest known value for Cd2+ detection) but also accurate for the quantification in high-concentration solutions with a detectable Cd2+ concentration of 6.0 × 10−2 M. Owing to the broad detection range, the CDs developed in present work show great potential applications in various scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of heavy metals causes severe environmental and sanitary problems all over the world [1,2]. Among miscellaneous heavy metals, cadmium (Cd), widely used in dye manufacturing, textile, and steel industries and as a component of batteries, is extremely toxic even in low concentrations [3,4]. Cadmium and solutions of its compounds can enter the food chain and bio-accumulate in organisms and ecosystems with a long biological half-life, therefore inducing a wide variety of acute and chronic lesions on human health [5,6]. Consequently, it is vital to have the capability to identify and quantify Cd2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Current detection methods for quantifying Cd2+ contaminants include colorimetry, electrochemical process, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and atomic fluorescence spectrometry [7,8,9]. However, large sample quantities, necessitating costly stationary equipment, and complex sample pretreatment procedures severely limit their applications in many real cases.

Notably, fluorescent chemosensor exhibits several advantages as a rapid, sensitive, and facile detection route, becoming an alternative optical method for the detection of metal ions [10,11]. During the past decade, carbon dots (CDs), which are discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm and display size and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior, have attracted significant attention in the field of analytical chemistry as the fluorescent chemosensing probes [12,13]. Meanwhile, the distinct advantages, including low production cost, chemical inertness, lack of optical blinking, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, endow CDs with potential applications in the detection of several metal ions [14,15].

High quantum yield and fluorescence intensity are the critical parameters for CDs to realize highly sensitive and selective detection of metal ions [16,17,18]. Many efforts have been dedicated to overcoming these challenges [19,20]. Doping heteroatom is an efficient and conventional route, rendering CDs ideal for strategically engineering analyte selectivity and sensitivity due to the tuned electron density and interfacial electric field [21]. Several heteroatoms, including nitrogen [22], sulfur [23], phosphorus [24,25] , and fluoride [26], were doped into CDs via “top-down” or “bottom-up” routes for the detection of Cd2+. Meanwhile, the detection sensitivity and selectivity improvement can also be attained through the surface functionalization of the carbon dots [27,28]. Various organic groups, like pyrene carboxaldehyde [29], phenolic [30], amino, and carbonyl [31], have been utilized to modify or passivate the surface state of carbon dots in the studies of the fluorescent detection of Cd2+.

Despite low limits of detection developed by the heteroatom-doped method and the surface functionalization route, the CDs probes only emit in dilute solution and always undergo serious quenching in detecting metal ions with high concentration [32]. As a result, these CDs are not viable strategies to reflect the fluctuation of Cd2+ with high concentration and are only suitable for the sense of Cd2+ in the dilute solution [33]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to design and develop CDs as new fluorescent sensors with a broad detection range that can achieve high sensitivity in detecting Cd2+ of low concentration in the dilute solution and strong fluorescence intensity even for industrial wastewater with high concentration of Cd2+.

It is still a challenge to explore CDs chemosensor for high selectivity and sensitivity toward both much high and low concentrations of Cd2+. Taken together, in the present work, the CDs were one-pot hydrothermally prepared using hyperbranched-polyethylenimine (HPEI) grafted by amides as carbon source, fabricating several CDs with different amdiation degrees as fluorescence sensors in detecting Cd2+ with varied concentrations. The functionalized CDs show good sensitivity in detecting Cd2+ in various water environments, including deionized water, tap water, lake water, and river water. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity enhanced obviously with the improvement of Cd2+ concentrations, regardless of the solvents, facilitating the exploitation of novel CDs chemosensors with a much broad detection range. Several characterizations evidenced that the interaction between Cd2+ and the grafted amides was responsible for the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity. These features rendered CDs potential applications for the detection of Cd2+ both in dilute solution and industrial wastewater.
The details of chemicals and apparatus used in the present work are available in Supplementary Information .

The CDs were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method, and the schematic presentation was displayed in Scheme 1 . The amidated-HPEI samples with different degrees of amidation were obtained by changing the concentrations of triethylamine and isobutyric anhydride in the initial solution, correspondingly preparing the HPEI, HPEI-IBAm0.65, HPEI-IBAm0.85, and HPEI-IBAm1.00 samples according to the amidation degree of 0, 0.65, 0.85, and 1.00. The as-prepared CDs weredenoted as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4, referring to the precursors of HPEI, HPEI-IBAm0.65, HPEI-IBAm0.85, and HPEI-IBAm1.00, respectively. The typical synthesis routes can be found in Supplementary Information .

Several CDs samples with different degrees of amidation (0, 0.65, 0.85, and 1.0) were tested to investigate the impact of the amidation process on the fluorescence properties. Typically, 0.5 mL of CDs-2 sample was introduced into the 2.5 mL deionized water, and the fluorescence intensity was collected after equilibration.

The quartz cuvette containing 3 mL of CDs-x (x = 2, 3, and 4) dispersion (1.0 wt%) was placed in the sample chamber, which was connected to a water bath with temperature control. Then, the water bath and sample chamber were heated to the set temperature with a ramping rate of 0.2 °C/min, and the absorbance values were recorded simultaneously by the UV–visible spectrophotometer at the wavelength of 660 nm using the deionized water as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.5 mL of CDs-2 dispersion (0.1 g/mL) and 1.5 mL of deionized water were added into 1.0 mL of Cd2+ solution with various concentrations ranging from 6 × 10−2 to 6 × 10−8 M. The mixture was allowed to equilibrate at room temperature for 2 min before recording the fluorescence spectrum using a spectrofluorometer. The fluorescent intensities at 460 nm were measured for the mixture in the presence (F) and absence (F0) of the metal ions. The detection selectivity of CDs-2 as a representative in detecting Cd2+ was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence spectrum of the solution of different metal ions (Cs+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Na+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Ag+). In this section, the concentration of metal ions was kept at a high value (6 × 10−2 M). The selectivity and sensitivity in sensing different metal ions were measured three times, and the average intensities were presented for accuracy.

To test the stability of the CDs-2 sample in sensing the Cd2+, 0.5 mL of CDs-2 (0.1 g/mL) and 1.5 mL of deionized water were added into the 1 mL of Cd2+ of different concentrations (6 × 10−3 or 6 × 10−4 M). After equilibration at room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum was estimated at different time intervals.

The effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-2 in the detection of Cd2+ was examined at a broad pH ranging from 1 to 8. The aqueous solutions of HCl and NaOH were utilized to adjust the pH levels. The influence of anions including Cl−, NO3 –, and SO4 2− was also tested by replacing the CdCl2 reagent with Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and CdSO4·8H2O, respectively, and the detection experiment process remained unchanged.

To study the feasibility of amide-decorated carbon dots for the detection of Cd2+, local tap water, lake water, and river water were collected and used as solvents following the present method. All water samples without pretreatment were mixed with different concentrations of Cd2+, after which the CDs-2 was added to these solutions. The standard recovery experiments were implemented in all water samples. During the recovery experiments, the ICP-AES was used to quantify the concentrations of Cd2+ before and after the detection test.

During the preparation process of the amide-decorated CDs (Scheme 1), the HPEI precursor was firstly amidated using isobutyric anhydride and triethylamine, leading to the amide groups grafting on parent HPEI molecules. Then, parent and amidated HPEI molecules were hydrothermally transformed into carbon dots, which can be classified into polymer carbon dots. To fully understand the surface/interfacial properties of CDs, it is crucial to closely examine the structural and functional group changes that occur during the amidation process of HPEI precursor. In Fig. 1
a, compared to the FTIR of parent HPEI, the strong band at 1640 cm−1 appears in HPEI-IBAm0.65 characteristic for the stretching vibration of carbonyl, and the band at 2960 cm−1 which are attributable to the C–H vibration of methyl group enhances obviously [34]. The N–H group assigned to the amine group shifts from 1571 cm−1 for HPEI to 1550 cm−1 for HPEI-IBAm0.65. Moreover, the isobutyl group splits into two groups and causes the appearance of stretching bands at 1241 cm−1 and 1300 cm−1, confirming that the amide groups are successfully grafted on HPEI. After the hydrothermal treatment, the band at 1640 cm−1 of the CDs-2 sample increases, and no obvious shifts of other absorption bands are observed, evidencing the formation of carbon dots and the stability of grafted amides as side ligands.

1H NMR is used to characterize the HPEI, HPEI-IBAm0.65, and CDs-2 samples. In Fig. 1 b, it is evident that the HPEI-IBAm0.65 has several signals between 3 and 4 ppm, which are attributed to the methylene protons attached to the amide groups, suggesting the successful amidation reaction between HPEI and isobutyric anhydride. The peaks belonging to HPEI-IBAm0.65 remain constant even after hydrothermal synthesis to form CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 c.

The actual degrees of amidation for the HPEI-IBAm0.65 and CDs-2 samples are calculated according to Formula 1 in
Supplementary Information .

In Table 1 , the practical amidation degrees of HPEI-IBAm0.65 (68%) and CDs-2 (67%) samples are similar and close to the predesigned value (65%), indicating that most of the amide groups from the isobutyric anhydride are anchored on the HPEI and keep stable in the hydrothermal synthesis.

Many dark dots with high dispersion can be observed in the TEM images of the CDs-2 sample (Fig. 1 d). The high-resolution TEM image exhibits that these dots own a crystalline structure consisting of parallel crystals with a lattice distance of ca. 0.24 nm, comparable to the (001) facet of graphite [26]. The particle diameters of several dark dots are calculated and marked in the inset TEM images, suggesting the uniform size of these CDs. The diameter distribution of the CDs-2 sample is also measured using DLS in Figure S2 . The diameter of the CDs-2 sample is around 6.02 nm, which is higher than that from TEM because the diameter from DLS represents the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs.

The fluorescence intensities of several CDs samples with different degrees of amidation are determined in Fig. 2
a. Compared with the CDs-1 sample, fluorescence intensity in the excitation range of 390–550 nm of the amidated CDs samples decreases. In the series of amidated samples, the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually with the enhanced degrees of amidation. FTIR, XPS, and NMR verify that the amide groups are grafted on the parent HPEI molecules and corresponding CDs samples. Relative to the amino groups of HPEI, the grafted amide groups are more hydrophobic [39]. Therefore, the amidated HPEI prefers to aggregate during the hydrothermal preparation, leading to the weaker fluorescence intensity of amidated CDs-x (x = 2, 3, 4) than the CDs-1 sample. Additionally, with the increase of amidation degree, the maximum fluorescence peak shifts to the lower wavelength due to the change in the surface state of the CDs caused by the different amounts of the grafted amides [40].

Aqueous solutions of the amidated-CDs dispersions are yellow and transparent. The photographs of the aqueous solution of amidated CDs samples are exhibited in Figure S3 . After being heated above certain temperatures, the solution becomes turbid and transparent when the solutions are cooled. These results show that the amide-decorated CDs samples are thermoresponsive. Fig. 2 b depicts the temperature dependence of the transmittance of aqueous solutions of CDs-x samples (x = 2, 3, 4) with different degrees of amidation, and apparent phase transition can be observed. As for the CDs-4 and CDs-3 samples, the absorbances decrease sharply at 18.6 °C and 25.2 °C, respectively. The absorbance of CDs-2 maintains constant from room temperature to 44.4 °C and then drops to nearly zero at 55.2 °C. Here, the phase transition temperature, defined as cloud point temperatures (Tcp), is recorded in Table 1. Lowering the degree of amidation effectively increases the Tcp value. It is generally accepted that more hydrophilic groups are closely correlated with the higher Tcp due to the higher energy required to release the hydrated water molecules [41]. The amidation process increases the hydrophobicity of the parent HPEI. Therefore, the CDs with a lower degree of amidation are more hydrophilic and have a higher Tcp. Nevertheless, the higher Tcp endows the CDs-2 sample wide temperature-sensitivity range, and it exhibits better stability even under high operating temperatures, making it a potential candidate as a fluorescence chemosensor.

To thoroughly evaluate the selectivity in detecting Cd2+, the fluorescence intensity variations after the introduction of various metal ions, including Cd2+, Cs+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Na+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, and Cr2+ into the CDs-2 dispersion are investigated. As shown in Fig. 3
a, only the presence of Cd2+ significantly enhances the fluorescence intensity. Metal ions such as Ba2+, Mg2+, K+, Cs2+, and Mn2+ have nearly no impact on fluorescence emission. The CDs-2 sample undergoes varying degrees of fluorescence quenching with Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Ni2+. The camera also recorded the fluorescence variations of the CDs-2 sample in the solution of typical metal ions, as displayed in Figure S4 . The CDs-2 dispersion containing Cd2+ emits blue light under a UV lamp of λ = 365 nm. As for the dispersion containing other ions, weak fluorescence can be observed in the Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, K+, Cs2+, and Mn2+ solutions. The influence of coexisting ions (Cr2+, Co2+ as representatives) is also depicted in Fig. 3 a. The co-presence of Cr2+ or Co2+ decreases the fluorescence intensity, while the fluorescence enhancement effect of the CDs-2 dispersion is still remarkable, suggesting the high selectivity of CDs-2 towards Cd2+. The selectivity may be ascribed to the fact that Cd2+ has a stronger affinity and chelate fast with surface groups existing on the CDs-2 compared to other metal ions.

The fluorescence intensity of the CDs-2 sample enhances gradually with the improvement of Cd2+ concentration (Fig. 3 b), demonstrating that the CDs-2 sample is very sensitive in quantifying Cd2+. No quenching phenomenon can be observed. Moreover, a great linear relationship can be found between the fluorescence response of CDs-2 and Cd2+ concentration in the range of 6 × 10−8 to 6 × 10−6 M (R2 = 0.9902), as shown in Fig. 3 c. According to the limit of detection (LOD) formula (3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank, and K is the slope of the calibration curve), the LOD of the CDs-2 sample for Cd2+ can reach 3.41 nM. Table S1 compares the present material with the existing results of previous reports. The amide-decorated CDs-2 shows good sensitivity toward Cd2+ than most of the reported materials. To the best of our knowledge, this LOD is the lowest known value for Cd2+ among the reports using carbon dots as the fluorescence chemosensors.

Moreover, in Fig. 3 b, the CDs-2 sample undergoes significant fluorescence enhancement in the presence of Cd2+, and the fluorescence intensity increases gradually with the increase of Cd2+ concentration. The quantitative concentration of Cd2+ can reach 6 × 10−2 M, much higher than the values reported in previous articles using carbon dots for the detection of Cd2+ [23,26,32,42] (Table S1). This typical feature enables the amide-decorated CDs sample with a broad detection range in the present work can be widely used in industrial wastewater with high Cd2+ concentration, which is also far from being achieved by other reported methods.

Fig. 3 d, 3e, and S5 show the time evolution of fluorescence intensity of the CDs-2 sample in detecting Cd2+ with the concentrations of 6 × 10−3 M and 6 × 10−4 M, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs-2 sample locates in the range of 390.09–400.94 and 356.67–367.81 for the detection of Cd2+ with different concentrations. Correspondingly, no apparent intensity change of these fluorescence spectrums is observed upon the detection time increase. Here, the ratio of F to F0 is utilized to reflect the detection stability of the CDs-2 sample. As shown in Fig. 3 e and S5b, little to no difference is observed in the fluorescence intensity of CDs-2 for the detection of Cd2+ of different concentrations, further evidencing its good Cd2+ detection reliability and stability.

Figure S6
a compares the fluorescence intensities of CDs-2 in the Cd2+ solution with different pH values, and Figure S6
b exhibits the influence of pH on the value of F/F0. Even in the solution with much lower pH values (1–3), the fluorescence intensities are similar, and the value of F/F0 keeps constant. As the pH value increases to 7 and above, the fluorescence intensities stay steady, showing great stability in various detection environments.

Moreover, this work also measures the fluorescence intensity of CDs-2 in the solution of CdCl2, Cd(NO3)2, and Cd(SO4)2 at the same concentration to eliminate the effects of anion ions. As shown in Figure S7 , no apparent changes in fluorescence intensity and F/F0 values are observed, demonstrating the negligible impact of the specific counter ion (NO3 – SO4 2−, Cl−) of Cd2+ on the highly-selective detection of the CDs-2 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The amide-decorated carbon dot sample (CDs-2) exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity towards Cd2+. Three actual water samples were collected and used as solvents to validate the applicability of the CDs-2 fluorescence chemosensor.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity increases gradually with the increment of Cd2+ concentrations. The linear relationship between the F/F0 values and the concentration of Cd2+ in the range from 6 × 10−8 to 6 × 10−6 with the R2 value of 0.9944 is satisfactory. The LOD of Cd2+ is around 3.39 nM, proving that this fluorescent sensor material has reasonable practicability for Cd2+ detection in actual water samples.

Lake water and river water always have more impurities than tap water. To further study the practicability of CDs-2 in the lake and river water are collected as actual water systems. As presented in Figure S8
a and S8c, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-2 gradually increases with the steady increase of Cd2+ concentration from 6 × 10−8 to 6 × 10−6 M, inconsistent with the results using deionized water and tap water as solvents. The linear relationships with the correlation coefficients of 0.9919 and 0.9932 for lake and river water are obtained in Figures S8
b and S8d. Therefore, the LODs for Cd2+ in lake and river water systems are calculated at approximately 9.40 and 3.52 nM, respectively. Hence, the amide-decorated carbon dots can ignore these distractions and retain high sensitivity and broad determination range in the quantitative detection of Cd2+.

The recoveries of Cd2+ in several water samples are also measured, as listed in Table S2. The CDs-2 fluorescent chemosensor exhibits satisfying recoveries in an acceptable range from 98.7% to 113.3%, with the relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) of three replicate detections for each sample below 2%. Based on these results, the amide-decorated CDs-2 sample as the fluorescent sensor for highly sensitive detection of Cd2+ could be used as an alternative tool in actual water environments and biological samples.

Fluorescence quenching often occurs when the CDs encounter heavy metals with high concentrations. In the present work, the fluorescence intensity of amide-decorated CDs-2 increases when it encounters a high-concentration solution of Cd2+. Therefore, an in-depth analysis of the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement is of great significance for the controllable preparation of carbon dots with special properties.

As shown in Fig. 1 e, after the introduction of Cd2+, the intensive distribution of dark dots becomes invisible, accompanied by the appearance of much higher dispersed carbon dots within the field of view. Correspondingly, the particle diameter of CDs-2 decreases from around 3.0 nm to about 2.3 nm, suggesting the possible effect of the Cd2+ solution on the disaggregation of the CDs-2 sample. To exclude the solvent effect, the same volume of deionized water without Cd2+ is added into CDs-2 dispersion, and the TEM image is presented in Figure S9 . Apparently, despite the dark dots being much dispersed in the view, the particle size of the CDs-2 sample remains around 3.0 nm, suggesting the negligible effect of solvent on the decrease of particle size. Compared to the amino groups, the amide groups are more hydrophobic. When the amide groups are grafted on the surface of CDs (Scheme 1), it is reasonable that the CDs particles become less hydrophilic. Therefore, the interaction between the less hydrophilic amide groups with Cd2+ is the main reason for the disaggregation of the CDs-2 chemosensor by changing the hydrophilic-hydrophobic behavior.

In Figure S10
, the UV–vis absorption peak of the fresh CDs-2 sample is centered at 364 nm. When the Cd2+ solution is added, no pronounced absorption peak shift is observed. The bands at 500–900 nm are absent, confirming that the enhanced fluorescence intensity of CDs-2 dispersion does not follow the surface plasmon-enhanced fluorescence (SPEF) mechanism. FTIR spectrums in Fig. 1 a of the CDs-2 sample are recorded in the absence and presence of Cd2+. The band at 1640 cm−1 shifts to 1615 cm−1 when the Cd2+ is introduced. Meanwhile, the -N–H group also shifts to the lower frequency, and a new band appears at 577 cm−1 assigned to the N-Cd2+ vibrations. These results suggest the strong interaction of N and O atoms of the amides with Cd2+. Based on the above results, the CDs-2 sample seems to follow the hard-soft acid and base theory (HSAB), which suggests that hard and soft acids prefer to interact with hard and soft bases, respectively [43]. The –N-H and -CO bonds of the amides, which are considered soft bases, likely interact with Cd2+ as a soft acid, forming a complex by providing lone pairs into empty orbitals of Cd2+. The developed complex causes fluorescence enhancement.

Nevertheless, combined with the structure of the surface ligands, the interaction between the Cd2+ ions and the amide groups forms a poly-atomic ring as the chelation complex following the chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. The formed bigger conjugate plane and stronger rigidity cause the structure to be more stable, leading to higher luminous efficiency [44]. The conjugated system also makes it easier to excite the domain π electronics to emit fluorescence [45]. Thus, it is reasonable that the enhancement of fluorescence intensity in the presence of Cd2+ is mainly due to the coordination of Cd2+ with O and N atoms forming the chelation complex with improved rigidity. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that the detection of the carbon dots will attain a plateau as the Cd2+ concentration further increases due to the fact that the interaction between amides grafted on the surface of carbon dots and Cd2+ will eventually become saturated. Nevertheless, the amide-decorated carbon dots prepared in this work still exhibit great potential for practical application in accurately determining the Cd2+ concentration over such a broad range.

In this study, a series of carbon dots with different amidation degrees were prepared through a hydrothermal synthesis route using functionated HPEI as a carbon source. Characterizations, including XPS, 1H NMR, TEM, and FTIR, were employed to thoroughly investigate the morphology and structural features of the as-synthesized carbon dots, demonstrating that the amide groups were grafted stably on the surface of the 2.3 nm-sized carbon dots and the amidation degrees closely corresponded to the pre-designed values. The amidation treatment endowed thermoresponsive features to carbon dots by altering the hydrophilic-hydrophobic properties, and the resulting carbon dots (denoted as CDs-2) with an amidation degree of 65% exhibited the highest cloud point of 44.4 °C. Moreover, the CDs-2 chemosensor showed high sensitivity in the detection of Cd2+ with a LOD of 3.41 nM and remarkable selectivity with a fluorescence enhancement effect, which enables it to detect the high concentration of Cd2+ (6 × 10−2 M), due to the interaction of Cd2+ with the amide groups forming a stable structure with improved rigidity. Furthermore, the CDs-2 chemosensor maintained excellent stability in the detection of Cd2+ regardless of the different pH values or anion conditions. Additionally, when lake and river water were used as solvents, the CDs-2 chemosensor demonstrated great sensitivity in determining Cd2+ with a LOD of 9.40 and 3.52 nM, respectively, and satisfactory recoveries. As such, future development of carbon dots chemosensors could include grafted groups (such as an amide) that can interact with heavy metal forming conjugate planes to selectively detect specific metal ions.

Lu Tang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Chenxue Zhu: Investigation, Methodology. Yingsang Yang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources. Jiajun Luo: Investigation, Resources. Jinhui Song: Formal analysis, Resources. Huimin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Suyao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yi Liu: Supervision, Project administration. Yiwen Fang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22072174) and the Start-up Funding of Shantou University (No. NTF22013). Financial supports from the Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (2111002) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515012115) for conducting this research are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
